ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai stir-fried chicken with turmeric/yellow ginger. FOODON_03302051 (chicken dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai stir-fried chicken with yellow ginger. A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Thai stir-fried chicken preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 150 g of stir-fried chicken with sauce and aromatics, served alongside 150 g of steamed jasmine rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken stir-fried with vegetables and sauce — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Thai stir-fried chicken dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai stir-fried chicken with yellow ginger exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for stir-fried chicken with sauce (NDB 35196 / similar entries) and FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for Thai stir-fried chicken, adjusted for typical turmeric/yellow ginger seasoning. Turmeric contributes negligible macronutrient change at culinary quantities. Per-100g values reflect the stir-fried chicken component including sauce and aromatics, excluding rice.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database reference for stir-fried chicken dishes with low-GI sauce components
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai stir-fried chicken with yellow ginger. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous stir-fried chicken with sauce entries from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate from sauce ingredients; yellow ginger/turmeric contributes negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 12.6) / 100 ≈ 5.7, where carbohydrate per 150 g chicken component ≈ 6.3 g × 2 = 12.6 g. GL reflects chicken component only, excluding rice.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a typical serving of this dish contains approximately 780 mg sodium from fish sauce and oyster sauce; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or limiting portion size is advisable to stay within the 2,000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 45 and high protein content (~16.5 g per 100 g), this stir-fried chicken dish has a modest glycaemic impact when consumed without rice; pairing with a small portion of brown rice rather than white rice is recommended to further moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load from condiments and moderate fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides a moderate fat profile (7.2 g fat per 100 g, saturated fat 1.8 g) which is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet, but the elevated sodium from fish sauce and oyster sauce warrants caution; turmeric/yellow ginger provides curcumin with potential anti-inflammatory benefit, though evidence at culinary doses remains limited.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Chicken with Yellow Ginger (Thai: ไก่ผัดขมิ้น, Gai Pad Kamin) is a classic Thai stir-fry dish featuring bite-sized pieces of chicken wok-fried at high heat with fresh yellow ginger (turmeric rhizome), garlic, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and aromatic herbs such as Thai basil. The dish is characterised by its vibrant golden-yellow colour imparted by the turmeric, a savoury-umami flavour profile with mild heat, and a tender yet slightly caramelised texture from the high-heat wok cooking. It is a staple of Thai home cooking and restaurant menus across Thailand and Southeast Asia, and is widely available in Thai restaurants in Singapore, including at Sanook Kitchen. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of lean protein (~16.5 g per 100 g) and provides moderate fat (7.2 g per 100 g), with the primary nutritional concern being its sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) derived from fish sauce and oyster sauce. Turmeric contributes curcumin, a bioactive compound with studied anti-inflammatory properties. The dish has a low estimated glycaemic index (~45) making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. It is dairy-free and generally halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified condiments. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein content, but sodium monitoring is important for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai stir-fried chicken with yellow ginger was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central stir-fried chicken entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for comparable Thai stir-fried chicken dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish composition is well-characterised and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: 1. The food_name has been normalised from the menu-style input '504D Stir-fried Chicken with Yellow Ging' to the canonical English name 'Stir-fried Chicken with Yellow Ginger'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases. 2. 'Yellow ginger' in Thai cuisine refers to fresh turmeric (Curcuma longa), not common ginger (Zingiber officinale); this distinction is reflected in ingredient synonyms. 3. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the stir-fried chicken component (including sauce and aromatics) and exclude accompanying rice. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this dish category. 5. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) based on USDA data for stir-fried chicken with vegetable oil; no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation. 6. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 7. Halal status is conditional on use of halal-certified fish sauce and oyster sauce; the restaurant Sanook Kitchen - Funan is not confirmed halal-certified. 8. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is a Thai dish; those fields are left null per multilingual rules for Thai cuisine (Thai native-script name is mandatory and provided).
